results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Alright team, big science day.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: In the lab next door, one of you will ride a tiny rocket through the digestive system.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Great question. We enter through the mouth, move down the food tube, pass the stomach, and reach the small intestine.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: What matters is knowing where it gets risky, and what to do next.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Bravery helps, but following safety rules matters more.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Talk to classmates and check room clues, then volunteer with real facts.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: It is unusual, but we control every step.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Being nervous is okay. Learn the facts first.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: You need a little more evidence before I open the lab door.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Talk with Nia or Theo, read the board, check my desk notes, open the shelf atlas, and check the wall clock.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Nice work. If you are ready, choose Volunteer.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Lab door is ready. Let's go.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Mouth entry is the easy part. The throat handoff is the risky part.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: The rocket must go into the food tube. If it goes into the airway, mission over.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Then speed matters. We can handle stomach acid only if we keep moving.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: No blind volunteering. Talk to me first.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Not yet. Bring me more evidence from the room and classmates.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: You already committed. Use the door.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: In the lab, you shrink, board the mini rocket, and enter through the mouth.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Stay out of the airway, cross the stomach quickly, then reach the small intestine to study absorption.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: That's the core lesson: be precise, not flashy.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: That answer matters only if you still choose this clearly.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Take a breath and choose when you are ready.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Lab door. Final briefing in one minute, then launch.

results.speaker: Nia Park
results.text: I know the diagrams, but it feels weird to map a real body.

results.speaker: Nia Park
results.text: Exactly. It's a real person, not just a school diagram.

results.speaker: Nia Park
results.text: Thanks. People forget this feels scary, not just like homework.

results.speaker: Nia Park
results.text: That is not how science works... I think.

results.speaker: Nia Park
results.text: Stomach acid is still the part that scares me.

results.speaker: Nia Park
results.text: The stomach has a mucus shield. We stay safe by moving fast.

results.speaker: Nia Park
results.text: Team reminder: respect acid, respect timing.

results.speaker: Nia Park
results.text: That actually helps.

results.speaker: Nia Park
results.text: Once you reach the small intestine, the real science starts.

results.speaker: Nia Park
results.text: Please do not make that real.

results.speaker: Nia Park
results.text: Still, good joke. Thanks for the laugh.

results.speaker: Theo Mercer
results.text: So the plan is to get swallowed on purpose for class?

results.speaker: Theo Mercer
results.text: Irresponsible and memorable. My specialty.

results.speaker: Theo Mercer
results.text: Ouch. Fair point.

results.speaker: Theo Mercer
results.text: Good question. That is the right worry.

results.speaker: Theo Mercer
results.text: That is why the epiglottis matters.

results.speaker: Theo Mercer
results.text: When you swallow, the epiglottis helps block the airway and sends us to the food tube. Miss that, mission over.

results.speaker: Theo Mercer
results.text: I still think 'learning by swallowing' is a terrible slogan.

results.speaker: Theo Mercer
results.text: True. Jokes are how I calm down.

results.speaker: Theo Mercer
results.text: But the throat rule is serious. Wrong tube means mission over.

results.speaker: Theo Mercer
results.text: Breathing and swallowing use the same space, which is bad design.

results.speaker: Theo Mercer
results.text: Epiglottis guards the airway. The food tube is our only lane.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Because you keep asking smart questions.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Because you stepped up and stayed to learn the risks.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Because you are scared but still thinking clearly.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Because you asked the right question at the right time.

results.speaker: Dr. Mira Sato
results.text: Good. Keep that empathy. It makes science safer and kinder.
